assumptions: The intragroup dividend has already been declared and recognised as an intercompany receivable/payable.
A recognised intragroup dividend receivable/payable is assumed to be analysed as a monetary item under usual IFRS practice.
The question is about consolidated financial statements of a non-investment entity unless stated otherwise.
The functional currencies of the group entities and the hedge documentation status are not provided, so those points remain open conditions.

approaches.label: General prohibition for intragroup hedged items in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedge accounting generally applies only to exposures with external parties. Intragroup items are excluded unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not qualify the intragroup dividend exposure in consolidation unless it fits an explicit foreign-currency exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 says only items with parties external to the reporting entity can be hedged items in consolidated financial statements, subject to limited exceptions.

approaches.label: Recognised intragroup monetary item hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify as a hedged item in consolidated financial statements if the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. A booked dividend receivable may fit this model if it is such a monetary item.
conditions: The dividend receivable/payable is a recognised intragroup monetary item
The counterparties have different functional currencies so FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The hedged item is reliably measurable
Formal designation, documentation and hedge-effectiveness requirements are met at inception
approaches.practical_implication: Formal hedge designation may be possible at group level, but only under the intragroup monetary-item FX exception.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk on an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements when the exposure is not fully eliminated on consolidation.
Paragraphs 6.3.1, 6.3.2 and 6.4.1 require a qualifying hedged item, reliable measurement, and formal designation/documentation with hedge-effectiveness assessment.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction cash flow hedge
approaches.reasoning: This exception applies only to a highly probable forecast intragroup transaction whose FX risk will affect consolidated profit or loss. It is not the right model once the dividend has already been recognised as a receivable.
conditions: The transaction would need to remain highly probable rather than already recognised
It would need to be denominated in a currency other than the functional currency of the entity entering into it
The foreign currency risk would need to affect consolidated profit or loss
approaches.practical_implication: If the dividend is already booked as a receivable, analyse it as a recognised item, not as a forecast-transaction cash flow hedge.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions to be hedged in consolidated financial statements.
Paragraph B6.3.5 limits this exception to cases where the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss; many intragroup flows do not.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge in a foreign operation
approaches.reasoning: The net investment hedge model applies to foreign currency risk on an amount of net assets of a foreign operation, not to a separately recognised intragroup dividend receivable on the stated facts. IFRIC 16 is limited to net investment hedges and should not be applied by analogy.
conditions: The hedged item would need to be an amount of net assets of the foreign operation
Net investment hedge designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements would need to be met
approaches.practical_implication: Do not rely on net-investment-hedge mechanics unless the exposure is the net investment itself rather than an intercompany dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge accounting model.
Paragraphs 7, 8, 11 and 14 limit the model to net investments in foreign operations, with the hedged item being an amount of net assets and not another intragroup exposure.
